name: New wine in old bottles
notes: The threefold synoptic account of the question about fasting, where the disciples of John and the Pharisees ask why they fast often but Jesus' disciples do not. The shared core is His answer in two movements: the children of the bridechamber cannot fast while the bridegroom is with them, yet the days will come when he is taken away and then they shall fast; and the twin parables of new cloth on an old garment and new wine in old bottles. Matthew has the children of the bridechamber 'mourn' where Mark and Luke have 'fast,' and where Matthew keeps the taking-away and the new bottles each within one verse, Mark and Luke unfold them across two, so the single verse is linked to both. Luke alone preserves the parable's closing word, 'No man also having drunk old wine straightway desireth new: for he saith, The old is better' (5:39), which the others do not record and which therefore reads unlinked. These are complementary vantages of one teaching, each Evangelist keeping his own faithful detail.
